business_info: The biohazard score is a composite toxicological risk metric assigned to molecules during laboratory screening.

In internal reports, medicinal chemists sometimes annotate molecules as “halo-rich”, “mixed-halide scaffold”, or “polyhalogen core” when several different halogen substituents are present on the same backbone; these phrases are purely descriptive and have no direct impact on screening workflows or query filters.

Some legacy registration files still refer to “organochlorine-like” or “pseudo-halogen” features, but these historical labels were never standardized across projects and should generally be treated as free-text comments rather than structured classifications.

Halogen substituents (e.g., Cl, Br, F, I) are catalogued at the atom level via the `element` column and may or may not be referenced in the free-text molecule `description`; informally, project chemists use terms like “fluorinated tail” or “terminal chloro” to call attention to these atoms during SAR reviews.

Computational toxicology dashboards occasionally expose a toggle for “halogen alerting motifs” that highlights specific structural fragments enriched for halogens; this visualization layer is not synchronized with the textual tags in the molecule table and should not be assumed to match any particular wording there.

For internal communication, screening teams sometimes lump together heavily chlorinated, brominated, or iodinated structures under shorthand labels like “heavy halogens” without specifying any cutoffs; this language is informal and mainly used in meeting notes.

The biohazard_score column aggregates several orthogonal endpoints (acute toxicity, genotoxic alerts, metabolic stability flags, and off-target assay readouts), but different discovery units have historically used their own undocumented weighting schemes when generating this composite metric.

Early-stage triage guidelines sometimes loosely refer to “biohazard outliers” as any molecules whose biohazard_score falls into the top decile within a given screening batch; this batch-relative heuristic is not encoded in the database and should not be assumed to correspond to a fixed cutpoint.

One internal slide deck describes “elevated biohazard” qualitatively as “compounds we would hesitate to store outside of controlled containment” but never states a specific biohazard_score threshold, leading to inconsistent interpretations between teams.

In historical toxicology reviews, reviewers sometimes color-code molecules as green, amber, or red based on biohazard_score quintiles for visualization purposes; these color categories are not written back to the database and have varied boundaries across projects.

The absolute range of biohazard_score is not contractually fixed; some campaigns have scores roughly spanning 0–100, while others compress into a narrower window depending on the panel of assays that contributed to the composite index.

Within cheminformatics discussions, some scientists casually talk about “atom-rich” or “skeletally dense” compounds when a molecule has many non-hydrogen atoms, but these turns of phrase are not tied to any particular atom-count cutoff in the database.

Internal slides on molecular complexity sometimes picture “high atom-load” structures as having more than 25–30 heavy atoms, but the caption notes that the threshold is context dependent and was chosen only for illustrative purposes.

The atom table is used as the definitive source for atom counts per molecule; hydrogen atoms may be either explicitly listed or suppressed depending on how the structures were originally imported from external tools.

In some cheminformatics notebooks, the term “macro-sized” is loosely applied to molecules that exceed 40 total atoms, but this naming convention was never implemented as a formal category in production systems.

A handful of project-specific SOPs once proposed labeling structures with more than 32 non-hydrogen atoms as “high-atom-load compounds”, but the corresponding controlled vocabulary was never rolled out to the master registration database.

Screening campaigns that focus on environmental persistence sometimes track an informal tag for “bioaccumulative halogens”, typically applied to bulky chlorinated or brominated motifs; this practice is handled in campaign-specific spreadsheets and is not mirrored in the core database schema.

Some compound triage checklists remind reviewers to “watch for halogen clustering on aromatic rings” and “extended polyfluoro chains”, suggesting potential flags for persistence, but they do not define any structured field or threshold for automated queries.

The carcinogenicity classification stored alongside each molecule was historically derived from multiple external authorities; however, not all of those source labels have a one-to-one mapping to current internal risk categories.

Not all molecules in the molecule table necessarily have matching rows in the bond or connected tables, because some legacy imports only registered atomic composition without fully resolved connectivity.

Bond identifiers such as TR001_A1_A2 are constructed by combining the parent molecule ID with placeholders for the two atom indices; however, this convention is not strictly enforced for all historical entries.

The `bond_type` codes used in the bond table originate from an in-house enumeration that distinguishes single, double, triple, aromatic, and several less common bond classes, but the exact code legend lives outside the database in separate documentation.

The connected table effectively normalizes the relationship between atoms and their bonds, but because it stores only text IDs, downstream users must join back to atom and bond to recover chemical elements and bond types.

Because atom IDs often follow the pattern TRXXX_i, scripts that compute per-molecule atom counts typically derive the molecule_id by truncating the atom_id prefix; however, the preferred approach is to rely on the explicit molecule_id column in the atom table.

During curation, some inconsistent spellings in the molecule description field (for example, “chlorniated”, “halgoenated”) were left as-is to preserve the original source text; these typos are not normalized into any standardized vocabulary.

Molecule descriptions may mention structural features like “linear chain”, “branched scaffold”, “single-ring aromatic”, or “fused polycyclic core”; these descriptors are free text, not controlled terms, and can vary significantly by data source.

Some external partners occasionally append project-specific tags like “priority for follow-up”, “stable salt form”, or “requires cold chain” into the description field when uploading new molecules; these are operational notes and have no standardized meaning in the toxicology schema.

A few data loads from historical environmental surveys reused the description field to capture sampling context such as “river sediment”, “urban dust”, or “wastewater influent”, which do not describe intrinsic molecular properties but were retained for traceability.
